title: Conjecture 2 (Alon [2])
statement_text: Let $G$ be a group of size $n$. The independence number of the random Cayley graph $G(p)$ is at most $\tilde{O}(p^{-1})$ whp.
context_text: It is natural to conjecture that random Cayley graphs behave similarly to random regular graphs of the same degree in terms of independence number. The best prior general bound was $O(\min(p^{-2}(\log n)^{2}, \sqrt{n(\log n)/p}))$ (Theorem 1). The conjecture is significantly harder to approach than its random-regular-graph analogue due to limited randomness and strong dependencies in the Cayley graph model.
paper_contribution: Proves improved bounds on the independence number of sparse random Cayley and Cayley-sum graphs, making progress toward the conjectured $\tilde{O}(p^{-1})$ bound; a companion follow-up work achieves sharp asymptotics for $G=\mathbb{Z}_n$ at density $(\log n)^{-2+o(1)}$.
notes: Reference [2] is: N. Alon, 'The chromatic number of random Cayley graphs', European J. Combin. 34 (2013), 1232–1243.
paper_title: Random Cayley graphs and random sumsets

title: Conjecture 15
statement_text: There exist collections of sets $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that [size/complexity conditions] and such that for every $|A|=s$ with $|A+A|\leq K|A|$, there exists $\ell\leq\log_{2}K$ and $F\in\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that $A+A\supseteq F$.
context_text: The authors believe the main covering lemma — showing sumsets of sets with small doubling contain dense subsets of low complexity — will have further applications beyond those discussed in the paper. This conjecture proposes an optimal or strengthened form of that covering lemma, and is motivated in part by a version of Lovett's question (from Green's list of open problems [14]) asking whether sumsets $A+A$ of dense sets contain large subsets that are iterated sumsets $B+B+B+B$.
notes: The statement text as extracted appears truncated: the clause 'such that and such that' suggests that the size or complexity conditions on the collections $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ were lost in the source extraction. The mathematical content may be incomplete or garbled.
paper_title: Random Cayley graphs and random sumsets
